ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly new architectural twist: a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and enforces acyclicity by deleting offending arcs, which the authors claim makes it the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses. It also develops an approximate dynamic oracle tailored to this non-projective, non-monotonic setting to enable training on reparable errors. These contributions are a non-trivial extension over prior monotonic Covington work (including Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) and would be informative to experts, though they build on an existing transition-based framework rather than introducing a wholly new parsing paradigm. | B(4): The paper presents a notable novel extension: a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and resolves cycles by deleting offending arcs, which (per the authors) is the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses. It also develops an approximate, efficiently-computable dynamic oracle for this non-monotonic, non-projective setting—addressing a non-trivial loss-computation challenge not handled by prior monotonic or projective approaches. While the work builds on the existing Covington framework and uses known evaluation datasets and baselines, the combination of unrestricted non-monotonicity with non-projectivity and an approximate dynamic oracle represents a clear and non-trivial advance over prior methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new architectural twist: a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and enforces acyclicity by deleting offending arcs, which the authors claim makes it the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses. It also develops an approximate dynamic oracle tailored to this non-projective, non-monotonic setting to enable training on reparable errors. These contributions are a non-trivial extension over prior monotonic Covington work (including Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) and would be informative to experts, though they build on an existing transition-based framework rather than introducing a wholly new parsing paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable novel extension: a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and resolves cycles by deleting offending arcs, which (per the authors) is the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses. It also develops an approximate, efficiently-computable dynamic oracle for this non-monotonic, non-projective setting—addressing a non-trivial loss-computation challenge not handled by prior monotonic or projective approaches. While the work builds on the existing Covington framework and uses known evaluation datasets and baselines, the combination of unrestricted non-monotonicity with non-projectivity and an approximate dynamic oracle represents a clear and non-trivial advance over prior methods.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles a meaningful problem—training transition-based dependency parsers that can non-monotonically repair attachments in the non-projective setting—by introducing the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses and an approximate dynamic oracle to train on repairable errors. Empirical gains over monotonic and static Covington baselines on a majority of CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets indicate the approach can change practical training practices. Computational and theoretical limitations (approximate loss, cycle enumeration costs, non-arc-decomposability) temper immediate widespread adoption but the work clearly opens significant new research directions for non-monotonic, non-projective parsing. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—handling non-projective dependency structures with a fully non-monotonic transition system—by introducing a Covington variant that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and enforces acyclicity by deleting offending arcs (claimed as the first non-monotonic parser for non-projective analyses). The development of an approximate but efficiently-computable dynamic oracle to train on repairable errors and the reported empirical gains over monotonic baselines on most CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets make this likely to change how researchers design transition systems and training oracles. The work opens clear follow-up directions (better/ exact oracles, integration with modern models) and is likely to be built upon within parsing research, though its broader impact beyond dependency parsing is limited by the oracle’s approximations and computational caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—training transition-based dependency parsers that can non-monotonically repair attachments in the non-projective setting—by introducing the first non-monotonic parser capable of producing non-projective analyses and an approximate dynamic oracle to train on repairable errors. Empirical gains over monotonic and static Covington baselines on a majority of CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets indicate the approach can change practical training practices. Computational and theoretical limitations (approximate loss, cycle enumeration costs, non-arc-decomposability) temper immediate widespread adoption but the work clearly opens significant new research directions for non-monotonic, non-projective parsing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—handling non-projective dependency structures with a fully non-monotonic transition system—by introducing a Covington variant that allows Left-Arc/Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and enforces acyclicity by deleting offending arcs (claimed as the first non-monotonic parser for non-projective analyses). The development of an approximate but efficiently-computable dynamic oracle to train on repairable errors and the reported empirical gains over monotonic baselines on most CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets make this likely to change how researchers design transition systems and training oracles. The work opens clear follow-up directions (better/ exact oracles, integration with modern models) and is likely to be built upon within parsing research, though its broader impact beyond dependency parsing is limited by the oracle’s approximations and computational caveats.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper addresses non-projective dependency parsing and introduces a novel non-monotonic transition system plus an approximate dynamic oracle, which are topics central to top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (modifying the Covington parser, training with a dynamic oracle) and empirical evaluation on standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets against appropriate baselines using accuracy are the kinds of methods and evaluation standards expected at such conferences. Its positioning and related-work coverage are appropriate for the NLP parsing community, so the work is a strong fit for a top-tier venue. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (non-projective dependency parsing) and therefore lies squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference. It targets the right audience by proposing a full non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser and an approximate dynamic oracle, and evaluates these methods on standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets against appropriate baselines. The problem formulation and experimental protocol (comparisons to static and monotonic dynamic oracles, use of shared-task datasets, accuracy metrics) match community expectations, and the work is positioned correctly despite noted implementation/approximation limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — the paper addresses non-projective dependency parsing and introduces a novel non-monotonic transition system plus an approximate dynamic oracle, which are topics central to top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (modifying the Covington parser, training with a dynamic oracle) and empirical evaluation on standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets against appropriate baselines using accuracy are the kinds of methods and evaluation standards expected at such conferences. Its positioning and related-work coverage are appropriate for the NLP parsing community, so the work is a strong fit for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (non-projective dependency parsing) and therefore lies squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference. It targets the right audience by proposing a full non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington parser and an approximate dynamic oracle, and evaluates these methods on standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets against appropriate baselines. The problem formulation and experimental protocol (comparisons to static and monotonic dynamic oracles, use of shared-task datasets, accuracy metrics) match community expectations, and the work is positioned correctly despite noted implementation/approximation limitations.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser across many CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets and compares against static and monotonic (including Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. However, key reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), the dynamic oracle is only approximate and some analyses were limited to the first 100,000 transitions, and no thorough ablation or sensitivity studies are reported. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental analysis lacks some important controls and full reproducibility information. | B(3): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive in terms of data and baselines (CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets; static and monotonic dynamic baselines) and report that the non-monotonic dynamic oracle outperforms monotonic on a majority of datasets. However, important methodological detail for reproducibility is missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and parts of the analysis are curtailed because exact loss computation/elementary-cycle finding was too expensive (only first 100,000 transitions analyzed). Ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., on approximation variants or hyperparameters) are not reported, though related work coverage is said to be adequate and several limitations are discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser across many CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets and compares against static and monotonic (including Gómez-Rodríguez & Fernández-González 2015) baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. However, key reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), the dynamic oracle is only approximate and some analyses were limited to the first 100,000 transitions, and no thorough ablation or sensitivity studies are reported. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental analysis lacks some important controls and full reproducibility information.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive in terms of data and baselines (CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets; static and monotonic dynamic baselines) and report that the non-monotonic dynamic oracle outperforms monotonic on a majority of datasets. However, important methodological detail for reproducibility is missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and parts of the analysis are curtailed because exact loss computation/elementary-cycle finding was too expensive (only first 100,000 transitions analyzed). Ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., on approximation variants or hyperparameters) are not reported, though related work coverage is said to be adequate and several limitations are discussed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported: they present a fully non-monotonic Covington transition system and an approximate dynamic oracle, and report empirical improvements over monotonic and static baselines on a majority of CoNLL-X/XI datasets. The authors correctly acknowledge and analyze key technical difficulties (non-arc-decomposability, cycle handling) and provide efficiently-computable approximations for loss, which is consistent with the claim that the oracle is approximate. Remaining concerns are limited to reproducibility and completeness of analysis (no code or hyperparameters reported, some analyses limited to the first 100,000 transitions and no mention of statistical tests), but these do not appear to undermine the main technical contributions or conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments comparing the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser (trained with an approximate dynamic oracle) against static and monotonic baselines on the CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets, with reported improvements on a majority of corpora. The authors explicitly acknowledge and handle the technical difficulty that the non-projective Covington parser is not arc-decomposable and therefore provide an approximate, efficiently-computable dynamic oracle; this is a reasonable and technically honest solution though it means some loss computations are approximate rather than exact. Experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines (including Gómez-Rodríguez and Fernández-González 2015) but is somewhat weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting/code and by limited analysis where cycle computations forced them to consider only the first 100,000 transitions for some analyses. Overall the technical work is sound with only minor issues (approximation and partial analyses) that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported: they present a fully non-monotonic Covington transition system and an approximate dynamic oracle, and report empirical improvements over monotonic and static baselines on a majority of CoNLL-X/XI datasets. The authors correctly acknowledge and analyze key technical difficulties (non-arc-decomposability, cycle handling) and provide efficiently-computable approximations for loss, which is consistent with the claim that the oracle is approximate. Remaining concerns are limited to reproducibility and completeness of analysis (no code or hyperparameters reported, some analyses limited to the first 100,000 transitions and no mention of statistical tests), but these do not appear to undermine the main technical contributions or conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by experiments comparing the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser (trained with an approximate dynamic oracle) against static and monotonic baselines on the CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets, with reported improvements on a majority of corpora. The authors explicitly acknowledge and handle the technical difficulty that the non-projective Covington parser is not arc-decomposable and therefore provide an approximate, efficiently-computable dynamic oracle; this is a reasonable and technically honest solution though it means some loss computations are approximate rather than exact. Experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines (including Gómez-Rodríguez and Fernández-González 2015) but is somewhat weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting/code and by limited analysis where cycle computations forced them to consider only the first 100,000 transitions for some analyses. Overall the technical work is sound with only minor issues (approximation and partial analyses) that do not undermine the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (static Covington, a dynamic monotonic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González (2015)) across CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets using the same accuracy metric. It clearly analyzes differences introduced by the non‑monotonic transitions and discusses limitations of the approximate oracle. Comparisons are largely fair, but reproducibility/fairness are weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of code, and some loss analyses were limited to the first 100,000 transitions, so minor omissions remain. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (static and dynamic Covington variants and the monotonic approach of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González 2015) across standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets using the same accuracy metric, and it analyzes how the new non‑monotonic operations and approximate dynamic oracle differ from prior work (including discussion of cycle handling and computational limitations). Minor omissions that prevent a perfect score are that code and hyperparameter details are not reported and the oracle is approximate with part of the analysis limited to the first 100,000 transitions, which could affect replicability and fairness checks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (static Covington, a dynamic monotonic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González (2015)) across CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets using the same accuracy metric. It clearly analyzes differences introduced by the non‑monotonic transitions and discusses limitations of the approximate oracle. Comparisons are largely fair, but reproducibility/fairness are weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of code, and some loss analyses were limited to the first 100,000 transitions, so minor omissions remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (static and dynamic Covington variants and the monotonic approach of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González 2015) across standard CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets using the same accuracy metric, and it analyzes how the new non‑monotonic operations and approximate dynamic oracle differ from prior work (including discussion of cycle handling and computational limitations). Minor omissions that prevent a perfect score are that code and hyperparameter details are not reported and the oracle is approximate with part of the analysis limited to the first 100,000 transitions, which could affect replicability and fairness checks.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and states its main contributions early (a fully non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle for training), and the methodology and experiments (datasets, baselines, evaluation) are clearly described in the summary. Notation and presentation appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with adequate related-work coverage. A minor presentation issue is that some technical limitations (e.g., the need to analyze only the first 100,000 transitions due to costly cycle detection) and the approximate nature of the oracle could be explained more clearly, and figures/tables are not mentioned in the summary so their effectiveness cannot be judged. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with its main contributions (the non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle) clearly stated early and a coherent experimental setup demonstrating empirical gains. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and the authors explicitly state limitations (approximate oracle, cycle-finding cost, truncated analysis), which aids clarity. Minor presentation issues remain: the summary does not mention whether notation, figures/tables, or examples are consistently defined and effective, and practical details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and states its main contributions early (a fully non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle for training), and the methodology and experiments (datasets, baselines, evaluation) are clearly described in the summary. Notation and presentation appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with adequate related-work coverage. A minor presentation issue is that some technical limitations (e.g., the need to analyze only the first 100,000 transitions due to costly cycle detection) and the approximate nature of the oracle could be explained more clearly, and figures/tables are not mentioned in the summary so their effectiveness cannot be judged.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with its main contributions (the non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle) clearly stated early and a coherent experimental setup demonstrating empirical gains. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and the authors explicitly state limitations (approximate oracle, cycle-finding cost, truncated analysis), which aids clarity. Minor presentation issues remain: the summary does not mention whether notation, figures/tables, or examples are consistently defined and effective, and practical details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported.

main_claims: Introduce a fully non-monotonic transition system for the non-projective Covington dependency parser that allows Left-Arc and Right-Arc to overwrite previous heads and enforces single-head and acyclicity by deleting offending arcs.
Develop an approximate (efficiently-computable) dynamic oracle for the non-monotonic Covington system to train on erroneous examples that need repair.
Claim this is the first non-monotonic parser able to produce non-projective dependency analyses.
Empirically show that the non-monotonic dynamic oracle outperforms the monotonic version on a majority of tested CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI datasets.
_summary.methodology: Modify the non-projective Covington transition system to allow unrestricted Left-Arc and Right-Arc actions that replace previous attachments and, when adding an arc creates a cycle, remove the predecessor arc to enforce acyclicity; train the parser with an approximate dynamic oracle that computes loss via efficiently-computable approximations (experiments used a perceptron classifier).
baselines: monotonic version by Gómez-Rodríguez and Fernández-González (2015)
limitations: The dynamic oracle is approximate because exact loss computation in the non-monotonic, non-projective setting is complex.

Computing some non-monotonic loss terms requires finding all elementary cycles, which is time-consuming; authors therefore analyzed only the first 100,000 transitions per dataset for parts of the analysis.
The non-projective Covington parser is not arc-decomposable, complicating exact loss computation.
